column1: Recommended pricing for products, variations, subscriptions, bulk-buy offers, and bundle roles. These are strategy recommendations that still need COGS/margin confirmation.

column10: Competitor / Market Anchor
column11: Reasoning / Marketing Tactic

column6: No default sale; use bundle/subscription instead
column7: Subscribe 10% at 45/60/90 days for repeat cases
column10: Natural Dog Co 4oz: $28.95 one-time / $24.61 Subscribe & Save; ManukaVet/PAW Manuka wound gel supports premium Manuka positioning
column11: Keep current price ladder because it already works as good-better-best. ResQ should not race mass-market balms to the bottom; the premium angle is Manuka/therapeutic positioning. Use bundles for value instead of cutting the hero price.
column14: Confirm exact jar sizes and landed margin.

column9: Horse care kit with Manuka honey or pet shampoo only if use cases match
column10: ManukaVet and PAW Manuka wound-care pricing supports premium wound/skin care space
column11: Horse customers buy by use case and size. Keep the same price ladder as pet cream so the product architecture stays simple, then merchandise horse-specific use cases separately.
column14: Confirm whether formula/label differs from pet skin treatment.

column10: Ingredient authority item, not a commodity honey listing
column11: Keep as a trust-builder. Pricing should support the premium ingredient story rather than compete with grocery honey.
column14: Confirm UMF/MGO certification and pack size.

column9: Pet Coat Care Duo / Hot Spot Rescue Kit
column10: Amazon best-seller set shows mass 2-in-1 Manuka options around $9.72; premium organic bundle brands use higher-value routines
column11: Keep premium price but make the shampoo/conditioner duo obvious. The single product is the entry point; the duo is the value path.
column14: Confirm bottle size; price per ounce should be visible on PDP.

column9: Pet Coat Care Duo / Hot Spot Rescue Kit
column10: 4-Legger and TropiClean both merchandise pet grooming as routines/bundles
column11: Match shampoo price for clarity. Pair by default with shampoo; do not make customers hunt for the matching routine.
column14: Confirm bottle size and whether conditioner has same COGS/margin as shampoo.

column6: Only use launch offers if compliance/margin reviewed; avoid permanent deep discounting
column7: Subscribe 10%, cap at 15% unless margin confirmed; 30/45/60 day frequency
column8: 2-bottle stock-up 5%; avoid aggressive CBD discounting until margins known
column9: Canine Calm Starter Kit / CBD Canine Wellness Combo
column10: Honest Paws oils start around $24.95 and use dog-size strengths; pet CBD brands lean heavily on auto-ship and solution pages
column11: Normalize pricing by strength and keep it consistent across flavors/names. CBD value should be communicated by strength, bottle size, testing, and usage guidance.
column14: CBD compliance, lab testing, and allowed claims must be reviewed.

column10: Honest Paws CBD bites and chews sit around $28-$35 and are positioned by benefit such as Calm/Mobility/Well
column11: Recommended prices sit right inside current market range. Treats are highly replenishable, so subscription and multipacks should carry the value message.
column14: Confirm count per bag and CBD mg per treat.

column9: Senior Dog Comfort Bundle / Treat multipacks
column10: Old Dog Cookie diabetic treats listed at $12 for 10oz; Amazon diabetic treat category ranges widely
column11: Set a clean one-site price and rely on value/ingredients rather than overpricing. This is a repeat treat category, so subscription and 3-pack options matter.
column14: Confirm bag size, ingredients, and allowed diabetic/low-glycemic wording.

column7: Subscribe 10% if it contains consumable CBD oil/treats
column10: Honest Paws uses solution collections and subscriptions; ecommerce bundling guidance supports complementary bundles to increase cart size
column11: Normalize the existing combo so it no longer feels arbitrary. Price should be roughly 8-12% below the component subtotal.
column14: Requires exact component mapping by dog size.

column9: Women’s Daily Repair / Dry Skin Relief bundle
column10: Amazon Manuka creams often appear around $16-$35, while premium organic skincare can price much higher
column11: Current ladder is clean and easy to understand. Keep it, then support premium value with before/after stories, ingredient proof, and use-case pages.
column14: Confirm current package sizes and margin.

column6: No default sale except routine bundles
column10: Premium organic skincare examples show high willingness to pay for anti-aging routines and bundles
column11: Use one unified price between the two legacy values. $69.95 protects premium positioning without carrying forward the confusing $60/$80 split.
column14: Confirm bottle size and active ingredient claims.

column10: Cleansers are routine entry products; price should not block first purchase
column11: Use the lower/main-site level as the unified price. The wash should act as a gateway into the skincare routine.
column13: Internal export pricing + routine ecommerce logic
column14: Confirm bottle size.

column10: Scrubs are routine add-ons, not daily replenishment anchors
column11: Price slightly above cleanser but below serum. It should improve routine AOV without feeling like a luxury barrier.
column14: Confirm jar size and recommended usage frequency.

column10: Men’s grooming routines sell best when simple and practical
column11: Keep the clean ladder. Men’s product pages should make the routine easy, not overly cosmetic.
column14: Confirm packaging and formula differences from women’s moisturizer.

column1: Men's Face & Body Wash + Shave Cream
column10: Dual-use products reduce decision friction and fit subscription routines
column11: Use the lower/main-site level. The value proposition is strong because it doubles as shave cream.
column14: Confirm bottle size.

column10: Serum is the men’s premium step, mirroring the women’s anti-aging serum
column11: Unify at the same serum price as the women’s serum for internal consistency.
column14: Confirm formula equivalency and size.

column7: Low subscription priority; reorder reminder only
column10: Men’s grooming add-ons should support the shaving/prep story
column11: Same price as women’s scrub. Position as periodic routine step, not daily use.
column14: Confirm claims around shaving/ingrown-hair language.

column10: Hair care is a classic replenishment category
column11: Keep current price but make duo/subscription obvious.
column14: Confirm bottle size and anti-dandruff/thinning-hair claim language.

column10: Pairing shampoo and conditioner increases attachment rate
column11: Match shampoo price to reduce friction.
column14: Confirm bottle size.

column10: Routine hair care can support auto-ship and simple duo offers
column11: Keep current price and build value through ingredient story.
column14: Confirm whether this is unisex or women’s line.

column10: Pairs naturally with shampoo
column11: Match shampoo price and merchandise as a set.
column14: Confirm bottle size.

column10: Baby wash should be trusted and approachable
column11: Use the lower/main-site level; baby category should feel premium but not intimidating.
column14: Confirm bottle size and baby-safe claims.

column1: Soothing Baby Diaper Rash + Skin Care Cream
column10: Natural/organic diaper balm competitors are often lower priced, but ResQ can use Manuka/premium story
column11: Lower the trial size to make first purchase easier, keep 4oz/8oz premium. Size naming should be parent-friendly: diaper bag / nursery / home size.
column14: Confirm whether zinc oxide, active ingredients, or cosmetics/drug rules apply.

column6: Use launch sale only on 300mg trial; avoid permanent discount ladder
column7: Subscribe 10%, cap at 15% unless margin confirms
column10: Charlotte’s Web and Lazarus show heavy use of CBD subscriptions/bundles; pricing varies by strength and brand trust
column11: Normalize across flavors. Keep higher-strength premium but make price per mg visible so the value ladder is obvious.
column14: CBD compliance, COAs, and state rules need review.

column10: Charlotte’s Web gummies commonly start around $34.99-$49.99 and bundles around $149.97
column11: Use the lower/main-site price to stay competitive. The pet-export $79 price is likely too high relative to visible market anchors.
column14: Confirm count, mg per gummy, and cannabinoid profile.

column10: Charlotte’s Web Stay Asleep CBN gummies are shown from $34.99 and sleep/CBD gummies are frequently bundled
column11: Keep premium over regular gummies because CBN adds specialty value, but avoid drifting too far above mainstream sleep gummy anchors.
column14: Avoid medical sleep claims; confirm count and mg.

column8: Buy 2 save 5%; topical 3rd-item discount only if margin supports
column10: Lazarus topicals promote buy-more-save-more; Charlotte’s Web topicals range near $24.99-$29.99 for lower/other formats
column11: This is a high-mg topical, so premium pricing is reasonable, but the current $79 should be tested against conversion and price-per-mg perception.
column14: Confirm mg, size, and topical compliance language.

column7: Low subscription priority; replenishment reminder only
column10: Bath bombs are ideal variety-pack and gift-add-on products
column11: Keep single price; use multi-pack to increase AOV. Scent variations should not require unique pricing unless COGS differs.
column14: Confirm scent list and whether CBD mg varies.

column6: No default sale until compliance reviewed
column9: CBD Wellness Routine only after compliance review
column10: Specialty cannabinoid products carry higher price tolerance but also higher claim risk
column11: Keep premium but conservative. Do not lead with weight-loss claims until compliance review.
column14: Highest claim-risk SKU; legal/regulatory review needed.
